question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
ground_truth: 10 business days with 20-30 business days extension
majority_answer: Under the Massachusetts Public Records Law (M.G.L. c. 66, § 10), agencies (Records Access Officers) generally must respond to public records requests within 10 business days of receipt. This applies to requests for Medicaid (MassHealth) records as well. The initial response must either provide the records, identify the records and any applicable fees, or explain why the records cannot be provided. If more time is needed, an agency may petition the Supervisor of Records for an extension, which can extend the response time up to 20 business days for municipalities or longer with approval.

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
ground_truth: 10 business days with 20 business days extension
majority_answer: Under the Rhode Island Access to Public Records Act (APRA), public agencies generally have 10 business days to respond to a public records request. This deadline can be extended by an additional 20 business days if the agency demonstrates good cause (such as the request being voluminous or requiring legal review). This general APRA timeline applies to Medicaid-related records requests made to the Rhode Island Executive Office of Health and Human Services (EOHHS) or the Department of Human Services.

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
ground_truth: 10 calendar days  with 14 calendar days extension
majority_answer: Under the California Public Records Act (Government Code section 7922.535, formerly section 6253), agencies must determine whether a request seeks disclosable public records and notify the requester of that determination within 10 calendar days of receiving the request. In 'unusual circumstances,' this deadline may be extended by up to 14 additional calendar days with written notice to the requester. Note that this 10-day period is for the agency's determination, not necessarily the actual production of records, which should occur promptly thereafter. These rules apply to Medi-Cal (California's Medicaid program) records held by the Department of Health Care Services, subject to applicable confidentiality exemptions.

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
ground_truth: 5 business days with 15 business days extension
majority_answer: Under New York's Freedom of Information Law (FOIL), agencies must respond to a public records request within 5 business days of receipt by either providing the records, denying the request in writing, or acknowledging receipt in writing and providing an approximate date (generally within 20 business days) when the request will be granted or denied. If more than 20 business days are needed, the agency must provide a written explanation and a reasonable date certain for responding. These timeframes apply to records requests made to the New York State Department of Health, which administers Medicaid.

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
ground_truth: 10 business days
majority_answer: Under the Texas Public Information Act (Chapter 552 of the Texas Government Code), a governmental body generally must 'promptly' produce public information in response to a request. If the agency wishes to withhold information and seek an Attorney General ruling, it must request that ruling within 10 business days of receiving the request. If information is not readily available, the agency must certify this and set a date and hour within a reasonable time when the information will be available. These general PIA deadlines apply to Medicaid-related records held by Texas HHSC, though specific records may be confidential under state or federal law (e.g., HIPAA, 42 CFR Part 431 Subpart F).

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
ground_truth: 10 calendar days  with 14 calendar days extension
majority_answer: In California, public records requests are governed by the California Public Records Act (CPRA). Agencies are required to respond to a request for records within 10 days. This response must either notify the requester that the request has been received and is being processed, deny the request, or notify the requester that certain portions of the request are exempt from disclosure. The 10-day period is for an initial response/determination, not necessarily for the full production of all records, which may take longer depending on the complexity of the request and extensions allowed under the law.

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
ground_truth: 10 business days
majority_answer: In Texas, under the Public Information Act, a governmental body must promptly release information that is not excepted from disclosure. If the agency intends to withhold information based on an exception, it must request an attorney general opinion within 10 business days after receiving the request. While there is no specific mandated number of days for a 'response' to a simple request, the 10-day rule governs the process for deciding whether information can be withheld.
